title: Move.toml pins PAS deps to branch `main` — non-reproducible build
description: Both `pas` and `ptb` git dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"` (branch name) rather than an immutable commit SHA. Any change upstream to main between builds silently changes on-chain behavior and invalidates audit scope.
impact: Non-reproducible builds. A silent upstream change to the PAS Policy API, witness requirements, request resolution semantics, or templates registration could ship with Hadron without review. Breaks audit reproducibility and makes incident response harder.
recommendation: Replace `rev = "main"` with the commit SHA currently pinned in Move.lock (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`). Adopt a policy of pinning by SHA and re-pinning deliberately with a code review per bump.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: register_pas_asset has no early check for is_pas_coin<T>() or is_coin_registered<T>()
description: `register_pas_asset<T>` does not pre-check whether T is already a regulated coin (via `admin::register_coin`) or already a PAS asset. The inner `pas::policy::new_for_currency` will abort with `EPolicyAlreadyExists` if a policy exists, and `add_pas_coin_caps` will abort with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` if the TreasuryCap DOF exists, but these surface as opaque errors deep in call stacks.
impact: Operators registering PAS assets get cryptic upstream aborts rather than Hadron's own error. Debugging is harder. If a coin was accidentally registered twice (regulated then PAS), the failure mode is confusing.
recommendation: Add early, local assertions: `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered);` and `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EAlreadyRegisteredAsPas)` before calling `policy::new_for_currency`.

title: Whitelist mutators on non-PAS coin abort opaquely inside DOF borrow
description: `assert_can_send<T>` and `assert_can_receive<T>` call `can_send<T>`/`can_receive<T>` which unconditionally borrow the whitelist DOF via `dynamic_object_field::borrow(&treasury.id, keys::whitelist_key<T>())`. For a coin T that is NOT a PAS coin, this DOF does not exist — the framework aborts with a framework-level error rather than a Hadron error.
impact: Any caller hitting the whitelist path for a non-PAS coin gets a cryptic abort. More importantly, future public functions layered on top of these helpers cannot gracefully short-circuit for non-PAS coins.
recommendation: Gate every whitelist helper with `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin)` before the DOF borrow. `ENotPasCoin` already exists in the file (line 67).

title: Hardcoded coin_registry address `0xc` in TypeScript register flow
description: `deployAndRegisterPasAsset` hard-codes the coin registry object ID as `'0xc'` and module package as `'0x2'`. While these are stable framework addresses today, embedding them magic-string-style in operation code makes network or framework migration riskier.
impact: Low — these values are stable across networks in practice. But any future coin registry relocation or testnet variation would silently break deploy.
recommendation: Pull the coin registry object ID from `CONSTANTS` or a shared `framework.ts` module that centralizes well-known addresses. At minimum, add a `// TODO:` comment citing the Sui framework address source.

title: mintPasTokens passes u64 amount as JS `number` — precision truncation risk > 2^53
description: `mintPasTokens(treasuryId, coinType, accountId, amount: number)` types `amount` as JS `number`. JavaScript numbers are IEEE-754 doubles — safe integers only up to 2^53 - 1 (~9.007e15). A u64 mint amount can exceed this and silently truncate during BCS serialization.
impact: Large-amount mints are silently corrupted. For institutional stablecoin bond tokens with 6-decimal fractionalisation, amounts above ~9 billion full tokens lose precision.
recommendation: Change `amount: number` to `amount: bigint` and pass it to the Move call as a `bigint` (the Sui TS SDK accepts bigints for u64 pure inputs). Mirror the existing treatment of `amount` elsewhere in the scripts.

title: No unit or integration tests for any new Move module
description: The three new Move modules (`pas_admin`, `pas_supply`, `pas_transfer`) and the treasury PAS additions have zero test coverage. No `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` helpers exist for any PAS path. Only pre-existing phase-1 test helpers (`create_for_testing`, `share_for_testing`) remain.
impact: Critical paths are un-exercised: whitelist add/remove, send/receive gating, mint with receive check, burn via clawback, seize flow, register duplicate prevention, template registration. Regressions during upgrades will not be caught.
recommendation: Add a test module (e.g. `tests/pas_integration_tests.move`) covering: (1) happy-path register + whitelist + mint + transfer approval; (2) negative: non-whitelisted sender/recipient rejected; (3) seize from de-whitelisted source to whitelisted target; (4) burn via clawback; (5) double-registration aborts; (6) paused mint/redeem blocks transfer and mint. Use upstream PAS test helpers where available. Run via `/move-tests` skill.

title: Template autoresolve breaks after Hadron package upgrade — requires manual fix
description: The autoresolve template command is registered with `approval_type.address_string()` which resolves to the Hadron package address at the time of registration. After an upgrade, the new package has a different address — clients auto-resolving with the stored template will call the OLD package's `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, which may still work but is an inversion of 'upgrade-first' semantics.
impact: Post-upgrade, transfers continue to call the old package. If the old package is version-blocked via `Version::check_is_valid`, transfers abort across the board until `update_transfer_template` is manually called by an operator with RegisterCoinPermission, per PAS asset T.
recommendation: Document in `pas_admin.move` module docstring that `update_transfer_template<T>` MUST be called for every registered PAS coin after any package upgrade. Add a runbook/checklist to the README. Consider a `bulk_update_transfer_templates(templates, version, vector<TypeName>)` helper to reduce operational load.

title: Single WhitelistManagePermission replaces spec's Add+Remove split
description: The Notion PAS Modules spec specifies two distinct permissions: `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission`. Code introduces a single `WhitelistManagePermission` that covers add, remove, set_send, and set_receive.
impact: Principle-of-least-privilege weakens: an operator entrusted only to add addresses can also remove or suspend any address. Spec-vs-code drift may confuse downstream integrators relying on the Notion doc.
recommendation: Either (a) split into separate permissions to match spec intent, or (b) update the Notion spec to reflect the simpler single-permission model. Document the rationale in the commit that introduced the permission.

title: approve_transfer couples pause-of-mint-redeem to pause-of-transfers
description: `approve_transfer` asserts `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()` — using the mint/redeem pause flag to also halt transfers. There is no separate `transfer_paused` state. This conflates two operational concerns: issuer halting new supply vs blocking secondary transfers.
impact: Operationally, a regulator pausing mint/redeem for an institutional event also halts all secondary transfers of the token. This may be intended but is not documented; if not, it prevents settlement activity while compliance work happens.
recommendation: Either (a) introduce a separate per-coin transfer pause flag and permission (`PauseTransferPermission`), or (b) explicitly document in the module docstring that mint/redeem pause doubles as a transfer pause. The second option is cheap and may be acceptable per design — surface the choice.

title: pas_supply::seize takes &Treasury (immutable) — inconsistent with burn
description: `seize<T>` takes `treasury: &Treasury` (immutable), while `burn<T>` takes `treasury: &mut Treasury`. Neither function directly mutates treasury (both go through policy-gated clawback), but the inconsistency is confusing. If a future change wants to mutate a state on seize, the signature must change.
impact: Readability / future-proofing. Not a correctness bug.
recommendation: Align signatures. Either both take `&Treasury` (if no mutation) or both take `&mut Treasury`. Given both are admin actions that might later emit aggregated state, `&mut Treasury` is the safer default.

title: token_template_pas uses same `module 0x0::token_template;` as regulated template
description: The non-regulated PAS token template declares the same `module 0x0::token_template;` as the existing `token_template/` module. Each is published as a separate package so 0x0 resolves differently on-chain, but the duplication hurts readability and tooling that inspects source.
impact: Low — per-package uniqueness is preserved. But a developer reading the repo may assume the two are the same file.
recommendation: Rename the module to e.g. `hadron::pas_token_template` (in `token_template_pas/`). Keep the regulated one as-is.

title: object-ID extraction by substring in deployAndRegisterPasAsset is fragile
description: The helper classifies created objects by substring-matching on the raw type string (`t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `::coin_registry::MetadataCap<`, `::coin_registry::Currency<`). Any rename or module namespacing change in the coin-registry framework will break extraction silently.
impact: Deploy silently picks the wrong object ID and the subsequent `register_pas_asset` call passes a bogus cap, aborting deep in the Move runtime. Debugging this from a script trace is hard.
recommendation: Use the SDK's `parseStructTag` or `normalizeStructTag` to compare canonical type identifiers. At minimum, assert `t.startsWith('0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<')` and `t.startsWith('0x2::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`.

title: pkg() returns empty string if deploy-output missing — no runtime guard
description: `pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both of which default to `''` if no deploy-output is present. Downstream Move calls receive an empty `package: ''` and produce confusing SDK errors.
impact: Running a PAS operation before deploy completes or when `deploy-output.json` is missing gives a cryptic SDK error instead of an early, actionable error.
recommendation: Add an early guard: `if (!CONSTANTS.package.latest && !CONSTANTS.package.v1) throw new Error('Hadron package not deployed — run bun run deploy first');`. Alternatively, log a warning at module load.

title: PAS events missing treasury_id — inconsistent with TreasuryCreated
description: PAS events (`PasAssetRegistered`, `WhitelistAdded`, `WhitelistRemoved`, `PasTransferApproved`, `PasMinted`, `PasBurned`, `PasSeized`, `WhitelistSendUpdated`, `WhitelistReceiveUpdated`) carry only `phantom T` and per-event payload, never the treasury ID. Multi-institution indexers cannot disambiguate events by treasury without re-deriving from the phantom T (which identifies the coin, but coin → treasury mapping requires an extra lookup).
impact: Indexers must maintain a coin-type → treasury-id index. Spec (Hadron Blueprint) says coin-scoped events use phantom T because T uniquely identifies the coin/treasury via the OTW — so this is spec-aligned, not a bug. Surface as observation.
recommendation: Either (a) keep current design and document the coin→treasury invariant in the README for indexer authors, or (b) add `treasury_id: ID` to the PAS events for indexer ergonomics.

title: full-setup.ts casts getClient to `any` when passing to PASClient
description: `new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` silences the TS type check via `as any`. Either the SuiClient type from `@mysten/sui` is not what PASClient expects, or the PASClient type signature is too loose. Hiding the gap behind `any` makes SDK-version drift invisible.
impact: Type-level guarantees absent; an SDK bump could silently break runtime.
recommendation: Remove `as any` and fix the underlying type mismatch. If PASClient expects a different client shape, extract an adapter in `utils.ts`.
evidence: const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });

title: register_pas_asset missing version.check_is_valid() at entry
description: `register_pas_asset` does not call `version.check_is_valid()` directly. Version gating is enforced only via the prior `new_treasury_auth<RegisterCoinPermission>` call (which does check version). If a future refactor allows auth proofs to be created without going through that path, this function would silently accept stale versions.
impact: Defense-in-depth gap. Currently there is no concrete threat — the auth chain does check version.
recommendation: Add `version.check_is_valid();` at the top of `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template` (which also relies on the auth-check). Costs one assertion per call; protects against future refactor regressions.
evidence:
public fun register_pas_asset<T>(
    treasury: &mut Treasury,
    auth: &TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>,
    mut treasury_cap: TreasuryCap<T>,
    metadata_cap: MetadataCap<T>,
    pas_namespace: &mut Namespace,
    templates: &mut Templates,
    version: &Version,
